title: Parallel Systems (Redundancy)

content.question: What critical thing has no backup right now?
sop: List every single point of failure, one supplier, one platform, one employee who holds a critical process
Rate the reliability of each critical element and its backup (see calculation, page 6)
For each SPoF, build one true backup, a second supplier, documented process, or trained backup person
Test the backup on purpose, not just in theory, simulate the failure and switch to it
Re-check quarterly; backups decay when nobody uses them
content.explainer: A backup only adds real reliability if its failure is independent from the original, two systems that both depend on the same server, the same person's judgment, or the same supplier's warehouse aren't actually redundant, they're the same point of failure wearing two names. True redundancy means the second option can survive the exact thing that killed the first.
questions: What critical thing has no backup right now?
If your single most-used supplier, tool, or team member disappeared tomorrow, how many days before it actually hurt you?
Which 'backup' in your business have you never actually tested, so you don't really know if it would work?

definitions.def: One thing whose breakage would stop everything.

content.equation_example: Rate each element's reliability as a percent. Primary supplier 90% reliable (10% fail), backup supplier 80% reliable (20% fail). Reliability = 1 − (0.10 × 0.20) = 98% out of 100%. Higher means both would have to fail at once for you to be exposed; lower means you're still one failure away from a real problem.
content.closing_statement: Untested backups are just backups you're hoping about.

caption:
Principle Nº 210: Parallel Systems (Redundancy)

Backups for critical elements. A backup only adds real reliability if its failure is independent from the original, two systems that both depend on the same server, the same person's judgment, or the same supplier's warehouse aren't actually redundant, they're the same point of failure wearing two names.

Ask yourself: What critical thing has no backup right now?
